confidence.disclosure: Jalal al-Din Muhammad Akbar, the third Mughal emperor and arguably the greatest Indian ruler of the early modern period, is exceptionally well documented in the official Akbarnama of his court historian Abu'l-Fazl, in the Muntakhab al-Tawarikh of his hostile critic Bada'uni, and in many other sources including Jesuit reports from the Mughal court. The major events of his life - birth in Umarkot, accession at Kalanaur, victory at Panipat, conquest of Gujarat, foundation of Fatehpur Sikri, the Din-i Ilahi, the conquest of the Deccan, and his death in Agra - are all firmly placed by year, and many by day.

sources: Iqtidar Alam Khan, Akbar and his Age

journey.event: Born on 14 October 1542 (5 Rajab 949 AH) in the desert fortress of Umarkot in Sind (modern Pakistan), where his fugitive father Humayun had taken refuge under the protection of the local Hindu raja. The Mughal Empire founded by Babur has at this moment effectively collapsed; Humayun is in exile, fleeing the Afghan Sher Shah Suri.

As an infant is separated from his parents during the chaos of the Mughal flight to Persia. Falls into the hands of his uncle Askari at Kandahar. Spends his childhood as a hostage of his uncle while Humayun seeks military help from the Safavid Shah Tahmasp I.

After Humayun retakes Kandahar and Kabul with Safavid help in 1545, the young Akbar is reunited with his parents in Kabul. Spends his childhood at the Mughal court in exile, learning warfare and horsemanship - but, famously, never learns to read fluently. Some scholars suggest he may have been dyslexic.

In July 1555, after twelve years of exile, his father Humayun returns to India and reconquers Delhi from the disintegrating Suri dynasty. The Mughals are back. Akbar, then twelve, is appointed governor of the Punjab.

On 14 February 1556 his father Humayun dies in Delhi after falling down the stairs of his library. Akbar, then thirteen, is in the Punjab. He is enthroned in haste at Kalanaur on 14 February 1556 (or, by some accounts, around 11 February). The empire is in fragments, beset by the Hindu warlord Hemu and various Afghan claimants.

On 5 November 1556 fights the Second Battle of Panipat against Hemu, the Hindu general of the Suri dynasty, who had captured Delhi and Agra a few weeks earlier. The Mughal regent Bairam Khan commands the army; the young Akbar is present in his first battle. An arrow in the eye fells Hemu mid-battle and his army disintegrates. The Mughal Empire is saved.

In March 1560 dismisses his powerful regent Bairam Khan and assumes personal rule at the age of 18. Bairam Khan is sent on the pilgrimage to Mecca but is murdered en route by an Afghan whose father had been killed by the Mughals.

In 1562 marries the Rajput princess Hira Kunwari (Harkha Bai), daughter of Raja Bharmal of Amber, the first major Rajput-Mughal marriage alliance. This becomes a model for the integration of Rajput nobles into the Mughal mansabdari system - one of the foundations of Akbar's empire. Their son Salim (the future Jahangir) is born in 1569.

In 1564 abolishes the jizya - the tax on non-Muslims - throughout the empire. This is one of the most significant acts of his reign and marks the beginning of his policy of sulh-i kull (universal toleration), which becomes the foundation of Mughal imperial ideology.

On 23 February 1568 takes the great Rajput fortress of Chittorgarh after a four-month siege. Despite Akbar's general policy of toleration, after the fall the women of the fortress commit jauhar (mass self-immolation) and the men sortie in suicidal saka; Akbar, enraged at the resistance, orders the massacre of about 30,000 of the inhabitants. It is the bloodiest single act of his reign.

In 1571, in fulfilment of a vow he made to the Sufi saint Salim Chishti for the birth of his son Salim, begins to build a new imperial capital at the village of Sikri near Agra. The new city, Fatehpur Sikri (the City of Victory at Sikri), is built of red sandstone and is one of the masterpieces of Indo-Islamic architecture. He moves his court there.

In 1572-1573 conquers the wealthy sultanate of Gujarat, gaining the empire its first access to the sea. After hearing that the Gujarat had revolted, in August 1573 makes a famous forced march from Fatehpur Sikri to Ahmedabad - 800 km - in nine days, surprises and crushes the rebels, and returns to celebrate.

In 1575 inaugurates the Ibadat Khana (House of Worship) at Fatehpur Sikri, where Akbar gathers Muslim scholars from different schools, then later Hindus, Jains, Zoroastrians, and (from 1580) Portuguese Jesuits, for week-long religious debates conducted in his presence. The debates radicalise his religious views and contribute to the formation of his personal religious synthesis.

In 1582 promulgates his Din-i Ilahi (Divine Faith), an eclectic spiritual programme drawing on Islamic Sufism, Hinduism, Jainism, and Zoroastrianism, with himself as its head. It never has more than a small group of followers - Bada'uni estimates 18 - but it becomes the most controversial religious act of his reign and the basis of later (mostly hostile) accounts of his alleged apostasy.

In 1585, due to the threat of Uzbek incursions on the northwestern frontier, abandons Fatehpur Sikri and moves his court to Lahore. Lahore remains the imperial capital for the next 13 years. Fatehpur Sikri is left behind, never repopulated, and survives today as a ghost city of red sandstone palaces.

In 1595 retakes Kandahar from the Safavids without a fight when the Persian governor surrenders the city. The Mughal-Safavid contest over Kandahar, the gateway to the Iranian plateau, will continue intermittently for the next century.

In January 1601 takes the great Deccan fortress of Asirgarh after a long siege, completing the conquest of the sultanate of Khandesh. This is his last major military victory. The Deccan campaigns of his last years are not as decisive as he had hoped.

Dies in Agra on 27 October 1605 (15 Jumada II 1014 AH) after a short illness, possibly poisoned. He is 63 years old. His son Salim succeeds him as the emperor Jahangir. Akbar is buried in the great mausoleum at Sikandra near Agra, which he himself had begun planning during his lifetime, in a five-storey red sandstone and marble structure unique in Mughal architecture. He leaves an empire that stretches from Kabul to the Bay of Bengal, with administrative, cultural, and religious foundations that will shape India for the next two centuries.
